coredata.dc:title: Influence of carbonized polymer dot (CPD) structure on mechanical and electrical properties of copper matrix composite
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Wenmin
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Zhang, Yi
Pu, Youfu
Zhang, Jiatao
coredata.dc:description: Outstanding comprehensive properties and promising application perspective make copper (Cu) matrix composite become a hotspot in the research. However, poor dispersion of the carbon nanomaterials in Cu matrix and weak interface bonding between the carbon and Cu metal are inevitable to be the biggest obstacle. Here, carbonized polymer dots (CPD), which has the structural characteristics of graphene and carbon nanotubes, as well as rich surface functional groups and water solvent dispersion, have been successfully synthesized via hydrothermal and employed as a nano‑carbon reinforcement in pure Cu matrix. Microstructure analysis demonstrates that CPD are well dispersed in the Cu matrix in the form of nanoclusters with a size of about 20 nm, and the interface between CPD and Cu is well bonded. The CPD prepared from EDA: CA (ethylenediamine: citric acid) 1:1 show ~20.0% higher in ultra-tensile strength than that of pure Cu and obtain ~97.2% IACS in electrical conductivity. Herein, our research will provide an effective way to solve the bottleneck problem of copper-based composite materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CPD
CPD/Cu composite
Mechanical properties
Electrical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the CPD/Cu composite powders

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52174345 , 52064032 ), the Yunnan Science and Technology projects (Grant No. 2019ZE001 and 202002AB080001 ), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project ( YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photos of the prepared CPD in daylight (upper) and UV light (bottom), respectively; TEM and HRTEM images (insets) of (b) CPD-0, (c) CPD-1, (d) CPD-2, (e) CPD-3, (f) CPD-4; the normal curve of CPD particle size distribution of prepared CPD (g).
Absorption spectra of the CPD (a), PL emission spectra (b), PL excitation (c), XRD pattern (d), Raman (e), FTIR (f) and 13C NMR of (g).
TEM image of CPD-1/Cu composite (a), HRTEM image of CPD-1/Cu (b), inverse Fourier of the labeled wireframe in (b) of (c-f).

(a) Stress-strain curves of the prepared CPD/Cu composites, (b) relationship between UTS and electrical conductivity of CPD/Cu composites and the N/C ratio, tensile fractography of the composites: (c) Cu, (d) CPD-0/Cu, (e) CPD-1/Cu, (f) CPD-2/Cu, (g) CPD-3/Cu and (h) CPD-4/Cu composite.
Mechanism diagram of hydrothermal reaction forming CPD, CPD unit, CPD cluster and interface with the Cu matrix.
Relative density, mechanical properties and electrical conductivity of the prepared samples.

head.ce:title: Influence of carbonized polymer dot (CPD) structure on mechanical and electrical properties of copper matrix composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Tin Group (Holding) Company Limited R&D Center, Kunming, Yunnan 650106, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Tin Group (Holding) Company Limited R&D Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Tin Group (Holding) Company Limited R&D Center, Kunming, Yunnan 650106, China)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Outstanding comprehensive properties and promising application perspective make copper (Cu) matrix composite become a hotspot in the research. However, poor dispersion of the carbon nanomaterials in Cu matrix and weak interface bonding between the carbon and Cu metal are inevitable to be the biggest obstacle. Here, carbonized polymer dots (CPD), which has the structural characteristics of graphene and carbon nanotubes, as well as rich surface functional groups and water solvent dispersion, have been successfully synthesized via hydrothermal and employed as a nano‑carbon reinforcement in pure Cu matrix. Microstructure analysis demonstrates that CPD are well dispersed in the Cu matrix in the form of nanoclusters with a size of about 20 nm, and the interface between CPD and Cu is well bonded. The CPD prepared from EDA: CA (ethylenediamine: citric acid) 1:1 show ~20.0% higher in ultra-tensile strength than that of pure Cu and obtain ~97.2% IACS in electrical conductivity. Herein, our research will provide an effective way to solve the bottleneck problem of copper-based composite materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu) matrix composites are widely used in machinery manufacturing, transportation, electrical engineering and electronic products due to their advantages of high strength, perfect conductivity, corrosion resistance and easy processing [1–3]. However, the growing demands of industry are driving the scientific community to design new composites with tailored properties for specific applications. Accordingly, the development of a new generation of Cu-based composites whose performance has been greatly improved has paramount scientific research significance and practical application value.

Carbon nanomaterials (CNM), such as carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene (GR) and their derivatives, have been extensively studied as reinforcement phases in Cu matrix composites owing to their inherent unique properties. However, fabrication of CNM-reinforced Cu matrix composites remains a challenge for many reasons. At first, the aggregation of CNM reduces the densification of the composite powder sintering process, which is unfavorable to the performance of the composite materials [4–6]; secondly, weak interface bonding due to the non-wettability between CNM and Cu will cause the low load transfer efficiency of the CNM as strengthening phase [7–9]. Thirdly, it is difficult for CNM to achieve dispersion and interface bonding while maintaining good structural integrity in the powder metallurgy process. So far, although many studies have conducted in-depth research and exploration of solutions to these challenges, and have achieved some results, there is still a gap between the ideal enhancement effect and the actual test results [10–13].

Fortunately, the appearance of the CPD as a copper matrix reinforcement brings hope to solve the above problems [14]. As a new type of carbon-based material, it has attracted extensive attention due to its unique core-shell structure. Abundant epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups exist on the base and surface of CPD. Due to the existence of these functional groups, the water solubility of CPD is excellent, and it can be further functionalized and surface passivated, the dispersion problem of CPD in Cu matrix can be better resolved by a suitable method. Moreover, amorphous C at the shell network is beneficial to improve the interface bonding between the CPD and Cu [15,16]. However, the CPD is mostly used for luminescent materials, few studies have utilized CPD as a reinforcing phase through powder metallurgy.

Therefore, in order to make full use of the inherent properties of CPD, we prepared CPD/Cu composite to solve the challenges faced by CNM in strengthening Cu matrix composites. Here, CPD with different microstructure characteristics was synthesized by adjusting the concentration of reactants. Then, CPD/Cu composite was prepared by ball milling and SPS sintering process. The Cu/C composites with good mechanical and electrical properties were obtained via low-temperature sintering. This research will provide new solutions and prospects for the development of CNM/Cu composite materials.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, CA) and ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, EDA) were purchased from Shanghai Mclean Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (China). All of the chemicals used in this work were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Electrical Cu powders (particle size 325 mesh, purity 99.9%) were from Shanghai Naiou Nano Science and Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Ethyl alcohol was from Yunnan Keyi Glass Co. Ltd. (China).

The fluorescent CPD were synthesized by a typical one-step hydrothermal method. In our study, five different reactant solution concentrations were adopted. CA is 0.5 mol/L, EDA is 0 mol/L, 0.5 mol/L, 1 mol/L, 1.5 mol/L, 2 mol/L respectively, which means the EDA: CA molecular ratios are 0: 3, 1: 1, 2: 1, 3: 1 and 4: 1. After stirring for 5 min, the clear solution was transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel and heated at 150 °C for 360 min. After cooling, dialysis and freeze-drying, the CPD powders were obtained and labeled as samples CPD-0, CPD-1, CPD-2, CPD-3 and CPD-4, respectively.

The ethanol dispersion of CPD and the electro‑copper powder were ball milled in ethanol medium, the ball-to-material ratio was 10:1, and the rotation speed was 100 rpm for 480 min and 200 rpm for 300 min. Then, the composite powder was annealed at 300 °C in a nitrogen hydrogen mixture atmosphere. Finally, SPS was used to densify the composite powder in a vacuum environment at 550 °C for 5 min.

Microscopic morphology and structure of CPD were analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2-TF30, S-Twin), UV–visible absorption spectra (UV1700), Raman Spectrometer (Raman, DXR2xi, Thermo Scientific), and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (13C NMR, ASCEND, 500 M). Phase composition of the samples was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, RINT, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. Surface functional groups were studied by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR, IS5). The interfacial microstructure of CPD/Cu composites was carefully studied by HRTEM. Tensile test was conducted by an AG-IS 10 KN equipment with a crosshead speed of 0.5 mm/min at room temperature. Electrical conductivity of the Cu and CPD/Cu composites were measured by digital conductivity meter (Sigma 2008B, Xiamen Tianyan Instrument Co. LTD, China). Fracture surface of CPD/Cu composites was analyzed by SEM (SEM, Nova Namo-450, FEI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CPD/Cu composite powders
ce:section.ce:para: Since CPD has good water solubility, we dispersed the obtained CPD powders into water to prepare colloids. Fig. 1 (a-b) show the color of different CPD hydrocolloids under sunlight and ultraviolet light. In order to get rid of interference of concentration, the same concentration of the CPD colloid was adopted for observation, and pure water under the same conditions was used as a control. It is found that the CPD colloids appear light yellow under sunlight, and the color become darker as the molar ratio of EDA increase. Under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, all five CPDs showed bright fluorescence, which may be due to the enhancement of radiative transition caused by the sub-fluorophore fixed in the CPD through covalent bonds or supramolecular interactions, i.e., cross-linked enhanced emission effect [17,18]. Furthermore, the color of the CPD change from light green to dark green and then to blue with the increase ratio of EDA to CA.

The microscopic morphology and structure of the CPDs are shown in Fig. 1(b-f). These CPDs are nearly spherical, and show the combination of the lattice fringe and amorphous structure, regardless of EDA: CA ratio, indicating different carbonization degree [19]. The interplanar spacing displayed by these lattice fringes is between 0.20–0.27 nm. It is worth noted that the lattice structure under the TEM observation is not only come from the lattice of graphite but also from the ordered and compact polymer frame structure called the supramolecular stacking structures [20].

The average diameters of CPD-0, CPD-1, CPD-2, CPD-3 and CPD-4 are 2.94 ± 1.01 nm, 3.10 ± 0.88 nm, 5.35 ± 1.52 nm, 5.53 ± 1.94 nm and 7.71 ± 3.00 nm respectively (see the size distribution in Fig. 1g). The hydrothermal generation process of CPD is very complicated. First, CA and EDA polymerize to form polymer clusters. During the cross-linking polymerization process, dehydration occurs, resulting in an increase in the hydrophobicity of the polymer clusters. Then, an initial carbon nucleus are formed owing to the microphase separation. After nucleation, small molecules or polymer fragments in the solution will further chemical adhere to the surface of the carbon core, obtaining an internal hydrophobic structure and an external hydrophilic structure. With further dehydration and carbonization to form a highly cross-linked structural framework, as the carbonization process proceeds, microcrystalline carbon regions and lattices appear in the interior. Finally, when the surface functional group of the CPD reaches saturation, the growth stops [14,17]. Obviously, the chemical equilibriums of the reactants, polymer clusters and carbon nuclei are important to influence the CPD size [18]. Here, we control the CPD size by simply adjusting the concentration of reactants due to their close relationship to the dehydration and carbonization reactions. The higher ratio of the EDA in the reactant means the more involved CA in the polymerization reaction, which is the reason for the CPD size increases with the proportion of EDA. Besides, the blue fluorescence is contributed from IPCA (1, 2, 3, 5-tetrahudro-5-oxo-imidazo [1, 2-α] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid), which is an intermediate product during the polymerization and carbonization. It can also act as a linker between CA molecules and a passivation agent for the carbon core [21].

Based upon the above analysis, the obtained CPDs by simply changing the ratio of the reactants have a degree of difference, showing different UV–vis and PL characteristics. Therefore, we have further studied the optical properties of CPD through ultraviolet-visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy.

In the UV-via absorption spectrum (see Fig. 2a), the peak at 234 nm comes from the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, which usually does not lead to fluorescence [22]. The absorption bands of CPD-0 and CPD-4 at 325 nm and 360 nm are attributed to the n-π* transition of carbonyl clusters [23]. This is also a source of fluorescence emission. Compared with the CPD-0, the synthesized CPD by using CA and EDA also exhibits the absorption peak after 400 nm, which is mainly because the surface state is one of the main sources of fluorescence emission [24]. Thus, the UV–vis spectra indicate that the fluorescent emission of CPD is caused by electronic states with well-defined conjugate aromatic structure and surface passivation.

Fig. 2b shows the normalized PL emission of CPDs prepared from different reactant ratios. Different from the normalized PL emission wavelength of CPD-0, those of CPD-1–4 are relatively long. Moreover, the intensity of the emission peak of CPD-1–4 shows a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. Correspondingly, the excitation spectra also demonstrate the same trends (see Fig. 2c). The correlation between the absorption and PL features has previously been specified as different conjugate networks and surface structures of CPD [25]. In this study, EDA plays a dual role of an N doping precursor and a passivator [21]. In the process of hydrothermal reaction, doping and passivation are carried out at the same time, the modification of CPD surface traps by amino and oxygen-containing groups tends to be perfect, which greatly promotes the PL enhancement. However, when the proportion of EDA: CA was continuously increased, the amino groups and traps on the surface of CPD increased. These surface traps can serve as capture centers for excitons and undergo nonradiative recombination, resulting in a decrease in PL intensity of CPD-4 [23].

Furthermore, the crystal structures of CPDs are characterized by XRD, a series of significant changes in XRD patterns of CPDs are observed (see Fig. 2d). CPD-0 has many organic diffraction peaks. CA will not react completely after hydrothermally heated at 150 °C for 360 min if there is no EDA participated in the reaction. These organics may be unreacted CA and some intermediate products, such as IPCA mentioned above [23]. Nevertheless, CPD still formed at this time, which can be confirmed from the results of PL and TEM. The CPD synthesized using EDA and CA under identical conditions features a low crystalline structure with a broadening peak, suggesting the carbon material with few graphitic sp2 carbon atoms domains [26,27]. 2θ values increase from 19.4° to 23.7° gradually then drop to 22.8° along with the EDA content reflects the trends of graphitization degree. In addition, the characteristic peak of organic matter in the XRD pattern disappeared after the addition of EDA, indicating that the addition of EDA reduced the crystallization temperature of CA in the process of intermolecular condensation reaction and induced the formation of eutectic.

The degree of graphitization of CPD is characterized by Raman spectra. Two peaks at ~1348 cm−1and ~ 1587 cm−1 are found in the Raman spectra of the samples, corresponding to disordered structures or defects (D band) and graphitic carbon domains (G band) respectively (see Fig. 2e). ID: IG ratios are 1.06, 1.04, 1.00, 0.95 and 1.03 of the CPD-0, CPD-1, CPD-2, CPD-3 and CPD-4, respectively, indicating a trend of increase before decrease of the sp2 domain size [28]. The trends of graphitization reflected by Raman and XRD are consistent, which indicates that CPD with different graphitization degree can be obtained by adjusting EDA: CA.

To understand the internal structural change in CPD with increase the EDA: CA, the chemical structure and compositions of the five CPD are characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). All these CPD are rich in functional groups, such as OH, NH, CH, CO, CC, CN and CO [29]. This is the reason that they have excellent hydrophilicity. Since there is no EDA in the reactant, NH and CN groups are not observed in the FTIR of CPD-0. The 13C NMR spectrum further proves the existence of chemical bonds (Fig. S2 (a-e)). For example, signals in the range of 0–50 ppm are assigned to CC, 50–80 ppm to -C ≡ C-, and 90–150 ppm to CC and 150–200 ppm to CO [30].

In addition, chemical bonding ratios of the samples are shown by 13C NMR characterization in Fig. 2g. The content of CO and CC bond in the CPD-0 sample is very high, which means that the CA polymerization reaction easily generates chain-like organic intermediate products without the addition of EDA. The CO contents are significantly reduced when the samples are prepared from EDA. The content of CC and CC bonds increases firstly and then decreases, which further indicates that the degree of graphitization of CPD is related to EDA content.

Fig. 3 gives the microstructure of the CPD-1/Cu composite. Observed under low magnification TEM, CPD presented in the form of light spots are well distributed in the Cu matrix with a size in the range of several nanometers (see Fig. 3a). We can find these light spots with small or large size in virtually every grain boundary, even some of them are discovered in the Cu grains (see white wireframe area in Fig. 3a). Although there are differences in the size of the light spots, the dispersion is still uniform.

After further magnification, we can see that CPD does not exist as independent dot, but aggregate to nanospheres with several CPDs, which can be regarded as “nano-cluster” (see Fig. 3b). These clusters basically exist in form of spherical or near spherical. In these clusters, some region has the obvious lattice fringes whereas some has not. Moreover, the amorphous region is in the center of the clusters, but the lattice fringe region appears concentrated at the edge of the cluster.

The CPD nanoclusters are formed owing to the unique structure of the hydrothermal synthesized CPD, which contains abundant of functional groups and short polymer chains on its surface, therefore, the self-assembly reaction will be occurred to obtain an organic network sphere structure embedded with nano‑carbon core dots [17]. During the hydrothermal of CPD synthesis and the subsequent SPS sintering process, amorphous carbon-network (AC-network) gradually formed from polymerization and carbonation of the organic network sphere [31].

The local enlarged view and Fourier transform diagram marked by black dotted frame in Fig. 3b are given in Fig. 3c and d, they also prove the aggregation and distribution of the several CPDs. The selected area diffraction (SAED) patterns of Cu (111) and CPD (110) in the Fig. 3d suggest the perfect combination of the CPD and Cu matrix. Meanwhile, a clear transition region between Cu matrix and CPD is shown between the CPD and Cu matrix. No impurities or pores are observed at the interface.

After Inverse Fourier transform of black dotted frame in Fig. 3b and further enlarging the transition region (see Fig. 3e and f), it is found that the lattice spacing is ~0.241 nm and ~ 0.233 nm, typically for the d-spacing (100) in crystal plane of the CPD. However, the lattice spacing of the matrix is ~0.313 nm, which is much higher than that of the d-spacing (111) in crystal plane of Cu matrix (~0.209 nm), this could be ascribed to the diffusion of highly active carbon atoms in copper matrix.

These active carbon atoms come from the CPD after the removal of functional groups at the high temperature. The highly active atoms near the Cu grains diffuse into the Cu lattice to be a well-bonded area, whereas the carbon atoms far away from the Cu grains form an AC-network. This is the key to the good interface between the CPD and Cu matrix, which not only guarantees the improved strength but also contributes to the extrinsic toughening.

Fig. 4a indicates engineering stress-strain curves associated with the samples. The ultra-tensile strength (UTS) of pure Cu is 260.2 ± 6.3 MPa, the elongation (EL) is 15.9 ± 0.7 when the samples are prepared by SPS at 550 °C. However, the UTS and EL of samples with CPD are very different from pure Cu. Specifically, for CPD-0/Cu sample, the UTS increases to 287.6 ± 3.1 MPa and the EL increased to 24.9 ± 0.3%. For CPD-1, CPD-2 and CPD-3/Cu samples, UTS and EL are very close, and the values are 312.1 ± 7.0 MPa, 34.3 ± 1.8%; 310.2 ± 5.9 MPa, 32.5 ± 1.1% and 309.0 ± 2.3 MPa, 30.9 ± 1.4%, respectively. The UTS and EL of the CPD-1, CPD-2 and CPD-3/Cu composites increase at the same time. However, UTS and EL decrease to 272.4 ± 5.9 MPa and 4.8 ± 0.7% for the CPD-4 sample. Combined with the previous characterization, it can be reasonably inferred that the CPD structure has an important effect on the mechanical properties of the composites. By varying the reactant ratio of EDA: CA, the surface states, core-shell structure and size of the CPDs can be modified. These will influence the formation of CPD nano-clusters, and then the combination of the CPD to Cu matrix. Hence, the mechanical and electrical conductivity of CPD/Cu composites will be influenced (see Fig. 4b). The mechanical and electrical properties of CPD-0/Cu, CPD-1/Cu, CPD-2/Cu CPD-3/Cu and CPD-4/Cu composites show an increase first and then decrease with the increase of the N/C (mol) ratio trend. The CPD-1/Cu composite obtain good mechanical properties and electrical conductivity at the same time, demonstrating that only the proper surface states, core-shell structure and size of the CPD can improve the performance of the composite material more effectively. In particular, the electrical conductivity of the CPD-1/Cu composite sample is as high as 97.2% IACS, whereas that of pure Cu counterpart is only ~92.2% IACS (see Table 1 ). This may be closely related to well interface bonding, N doping and surface state of the CPD.

Fracture morphologies of the composites after being stretched are given in Fig. 4 (c-h). The tensile fracture morphology of pure Cu is shown in Fig. 4c, many nearly round dimples of varying sizes have been observed. Different from pure Cu. After adding CPD, the torn ridges were obviously observed, especially in the Fig. 4(d-g), and the dimples were uneven in size and depth, which was also one of the main reasons for its good toughness. Remarkably, the ports in both Fig. 4(d-e) show elongation associated with the force direction and exhibit many tear ridges. In addition, the fracture in Fig. 4e shows many nano-scale tough nest areas. However, the fracture morphology in Fig. 4h tends to be lamellar structure. Which owing to the development of microcracks during tensile testing pile up of dislocation and stress concentration around the reinforced phase can decrease effectively ductility of the composite [32].

Strengthening mechanisms, including dislocation strengthening, grain boundary strengthening and load transfer are usually considered for a composite. In the case of CCu system, the solid solution effect can be negligible owing to the very limited solution of C particles in the Cu matrix [33]. According to our previous studies, the main strengthening mechanism in CPD/Cu composites is load transfer [34], which is similar with the CNT and RGO reinforced Cu matrix composites [12,13]. Hence, the structure of the CPD itself, dispersion state and bonding state with copper will affect the efficiency of the load transfer.

As mentioned above, the formation of the CPD is due to the dehydration condensation reaction and decarburization reaction between CA and EDA. The addition of EDA will aggravate the degree of polymerization, thereby increase the size of the CPD and the content of the N atoms (including doped N in the core and -NH2 on the surface) in the CPD at the same time. Hence, self-assembly reactions are easy to occur between the CPD due to a large number of functional groups on their surface. According to the size and surface groups of the CPD unit, CPD nanoclusters with different network and core density are obtained. As the main existing formation of the CPD in the matrix, load transfer is actually depending on the structure, dispersion and bonding state with copper of the CPD nano-clusters.

The CPD clusters are uniformly dispersed in the Cu matrix, and the interface between the CPD clusters and Cu is well transited. Take CPD-1/Cu composite as an example to evaluate the contribution of the different strengthening mechanisms. According to Eq. S3 [35], the load transfer contributes to the strengthening effect is ~45.75 MPa. The dislocation strengthening value is calculated according to Eq. S2 is ~31.65 MPa [36]. The value of fine grain contribution calculated by Hall-Petch (see Eq. S1) [37,38] is ~10.72 MPa. The load transfer still plays an important role in strengthening the Cu matrix as CNT, RGO etc. reinforcements.

Besides, high load transfer efficiency is related to the strength, dispersion and interface bonding of the CPD nanoclusters. With the change of the ratio of EDA: CA from 0: 3 to 4: 1, the relative proportion of nano clusters and nitrogen content in the CPD unit is increased, leading to a different interface combination with the Cu matrix. From the mechanical analysis results, it can be seen that the ratio of N: C reactant of the CPD certainly have an impact in the expected macroscopic properties of the material, especially when the ratio is too large or too small. Hence, a schematic diagram is given as shown in Fig. 5 based on the microstructure of the CPD with different ratio of N: C reactant.

When there is no EDA in the reactant, CPD can also be generated. However, due to the lack of reaction accelerators, the size of the CPD is very small, and the organic molecular chains on their surface are very short. Therefore, during the sintering with the copper matrix, the transition zone is small and the coordination ability of the ac network is weak, which in turn causes lower enhancement effect. Furthermore, when EDA is involved in the reactants, the polycondensation reaction will increase significantly, and the CPD size will increase significantly, meanwhile, the number and length of molecular chains on their surface are relatively moderate, resulting in an effective interface transition and excellent bonding with the copper matrix. This is the reason that the ultra-tensile strength and elongation of composites can be maintained at a high level when the concentration of EDA: CA changes in the interval of 1: 1–3: 1.

However, when the EDA in the reactant is excessive, the CPD will become very large, excessive growth consumes many organic carbon sources, resulting in the reduction of the organic chain. The transition zone will also be reduced owing to the limited active carbon atoms, the coordination with the copper interface will also be weakened at the same time. Hence, the mechanical properties of composite materials will also be significantly reduced as shown by the test results.

For electrical conductivity, similarly, the concentration and distribution of the CPD and CPD nano-clusters will have an important effect on the conductivity of CPD/Cu composites. These effects will ultimately be reflected in the electron travel distance, namely electron mean free path. The electrical conductivity of the composites increases with the mean free path of electrons. It is well known that N-doping is beneficial for the electrical conductivity of the CPD, because which can increase the charge mobility and optimize the electronic structure [39]. As shown in Fig. 5, good interface bonding, whether it is the lattice coordination between the AC carbon network and the copper interface or the diffusion of highly active carbon in the copper lattice, is conducive to the transmission of electrons. As shown in Table 1, the electrical conductivity of the CPD/Cu composite is affected by the structures of the CPD. This is attributed to the effect of CPD structure on CPD-Cu interfacial scattering, and it is responsible that the mean free path of electrons will be reduced by interface scattering. Herein, from Fuchs size effect theory, the contribution of interface scattering to the resistivity can be calculated as [40]:
(1)
ρ =
1 +
3 16
1 − P
λ 0
T
L S
ρ 0
T
where P is the probability of elastic scattering at the interface, λ0 is the bulk mean free path of electrons, ρ0 is the bulk resistivity, T is temperature, L is perimeter and S is cross-sectional area of the phase. The term (1-P) is the probability of inelastic scattering at interfaces that is responsible for decreased conductivity. P is directly affected by the interface morphology according to Fuchs. The L/S represents the interface area per unit volume of the phase. According to formula (1), the interface resistance between Cu and CPD depends on how the interface were connected, which means the appropriate infiltrating between the two phases interface, the strong interface connection it has. In addition, the more interface area has the same effect as well.

As shown in Fig. 5, the interface bonding between CPD and Cu is divided into three categories. When an appropriate amount of EDA (EDA: CA = 1: 1–3: 1) participates in the reaction, the polymer was synthesized on the surface of CPD, which has appropriate molecular weight. In addition, the activated carbon atoms present in the CPD will rapidly diffuse into the adjacent Cu grains during the subsequent SPS sintering process, forming a transition area of about 5 nm (see Fig. 3(c)), the structure of transition area is similar to covetics structure [41]. In the Cu covetics, CPD and Cu arrangement with the assumption that the distance between gaps in (111) Cu surface equals to the distance between the CPD atoms. Remarkably, the nanocarbons and metal in a bond i.e., stronger than sp2 in the Cu covetic [42–44], which implies that the good covalent bond in the covetic interface was formed between Cu atoms and C atoms, and the contact resistance between CPD and Cu will be reduced. In contrast, when EDA: CA is 0: 3 or 4: 1, the transition region formed is relatively reduced. Combined with formula (1), when CPD-1, 2, 3 were added, the interface resistance of the CPD/Cu composite will be reduced due to the tight interface connection between CPD and Cu, and the more effective interface formation. The interface resistance of CPD/Cu interface can be easily regulated by adjusting the structures of CPD. Therefore, the overall electrical conductivity can be improved by increasing the P and L/S values.

The strengthening behaviors of the CPD/Cu composites with different structures of CPD were investigated by controlling the solvent concentration in the hydrothermal reaction. The structures of CPD, including size and surface state, the interface bonding between CPD and Cu is directly affected. We believed the appropriated size and the surface state of CPD are vital factors of synthesize CPD/Cu composites that has ideal traits. Correspondingly, the UTS (increased by ~20%), EL (increased by ~113%) and electrical conductivity (increased by ~5%) of the CPD-1/Cu composite reached the maximum. The improved UTS and electrical conductivity in the CPD-1/Cu composites possessing tight CPD-Cu interfaces and more effectively interfaces are found to be mainly due to the increase in load transfer efficiency and the electron transmission paths in the interface, which is benefiting from the unique structure of CPD-1. This strategy of introducing structurally adjustable CPD can be applied in Cu matrix by powder metallurgy with outstanding mechanical and electrical properties is of great research significance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Chinese National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 52174345, 52064032), the Yunnan Science and Technology projects (Grant No. 2019ZE001 and 202002AB080001), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchar.2021.111463.
